correct_explanation: JavaScript bundles are fully readable by any user via browser dev tools, and HTTPS traffic can be inspected by the requester themselves — there is no technical barrier to extracting an embedded API key. Once extracted, the key grants unrestricted API access billed to the owner with no way to scope requests, rate-limit per user, or distinguish legitimate traffic from abuse. A server-side proxy keeps the key out of the client entirely and adds the only enforcement layer that can validate requests before forwarding them to Anthropic.

distractor_explanations.A: Key rotation without a frontend redeploy is a real operational benefit, but it is a secondary convenience — not the primary security requirement. The core problem is unauthorized access by any user who can read the bundle, which rotation alone does not prevent.
distractor_explanations.C: Build-time environment variables are injected as plaintext strings into the compiled bundle before it is served to the browser, making them just as exposed as a hardcoded string. The distinction between 'environment variable' and 'hardcoded' is a development-time concept that disappears at the output artifact level.
distractor_explanations.D: The Anthropic API does not enforce same-origin restrictions — browsers can make cross-origin requests to it directly. The prohibition on client-side keys is a security policy, not an API-enforced technical restriction.

correct_explanation: The system prompt is the canonical place to define persistent behavioral contracts — format, length, tone, and audience — because it applies uniformly to every interaction without per-request overhead or fragmentation. The root cause here is an underspecified system prompt that leaves Claude to infer output shape from query complexity alone, producing the observed variance. Adding explicit constraints eliminates the ambiguity at the source rather than patching symptoms downstream.

distractor_explanations.A: Prepending instructions per user message distributes a system-level concern across every request, creating fragmentation: any message that bypasses the injection layer loses the constraint, and it adds latency and complexity without fixing the underspecified system prompt.
distractor_explanations.C: The retry loop treats a prompt engineering failure as a runtime correction problem, doubling latency on long responses and adding brittle logic — it does not teach Claude what the desired format is, so variance persists and the loop fires unpredictably.
distractor_explanations.D: Fine-tuning is disproportionate for a formatting problem solvable with prompt constraints, requires curated training data and retraining cycles, and loses the ability to adjust behavior without another fine-tuning run — the wrong tool for a runtime specification problem.

correct_explanation: Format inconsistency originates in the system prompt's lack of explicit constraints—Claude infers format from context when none are provided, producing variable output. Specifying sentence count, prose style, and reading level in the system prompt establishes a consistent behavioral contract applied to every request before any user message is processed. This addresses the root cause rather than patching symptoms downstream.

distractor_explanations.A: Temperature controls output randomness, not response length or formatting style. Setting temperature=0 makes token selection deterministic but does nothing to constrain structure, prose format, or length variance.
distractor_explanations.C: Retry logic patches inconsistent output after the fact rather than preventing it. This adds latency, increases API costs, and still produces non-conforming first responses that must be discarded.
distractor_explanations.D: max_tokens imposes a hard token ceiling that truncates responses mid-sentence on complex queries rather than shaping appropriate length. It also does nothing to suppress markdown or standardize prose format.

correct_explanation: Streaming solves the abandonment problem at the architectural root: it breaks the synchronous block-and-deliver model so the client renders tokens as they arrive, delivering visible output within ~1 second regardless of total response length. The data shows users abandon between 8-12 seconds waiting for a blank screen to resolve — streaming collapses that perceived wait to near-zero. No UI trick achieves this because the actual latency is unchanged; streaming changes the delivery contract itself.

distractor_explanations.B: Spinners and placeholders mask latency without reducing it — users still wait 8-12 seconds for real content, which is exactly the abandonment window the data identifies. UI affordances raise tolerance slightly but cannot overcome a 10-second blank response window.
distractor_explanations.C: Capping max_tokens degrades the product to paper over an architectural problem — the writing assistant's value depends on 500-1500 token outputs, and synchronous delivery of even 300 tokens can exceed 5 seconds under load. This trades product quality for a workaround that doesn't scale.
distractor_explanations.D: A WebSocket connection that still buffers the full response before forwarding eliminates HTTP overhead but preserves the full synchronous wait — the client still receives nothing until generation is complete. This conflates transport-layer optimization with streaming, which is an application-layer concern.
